query: Polymarket Pulse vertical short about manual tab scanning pain, dark terminal, branded, 5 seconds, no hype, signal-first
material_summary: Audience pain: by the time you notice a move, it is already old. Real market data overlay with prev->now, one market card, one line: workflow too manual, final button to @polymarket_pulse_bot. Palette #0d0f0e #131714 #1e2520 #e8ede9 #00ff88.

selected_style.name: Seed Remix Default
selected_style.one_sentence_direction: Make the remix feel like an AI briefing system running out of context while trying to stay polite.
selected_style.palette: Midnight navy, terminal green, pale violet, cold blue, warning red, soft gray.
selected_style.typography: Monospaced primary type with compact sans-serif labels.
selected_style.motion: Prompt-response stacking, subtle scanline crawl, deadpan holds, cursor blinks, soft zoom ruptures.
selected_style.transitions: Signal loss cards, channel switches, bug overlays, scanline punches, blackout frames.
selected_style.audio: Server hum, breathy synthetic lines, tiny confirmation clicks, dropout silences, low pulse.
signature_gags: borrowed visual seriousness colliding with the wrong emotional register
different systems of presentation interrupting each other on purpose
one recurring motif mutating as if multiple editors touched the same short
a cheerful assistant tone undercut by crisis subtitles
system notes revealing what the main text is hiding
anti_patterns: Do not let the seed remix become a checklist of borrowed tricks.
Do not sample so many ingredients that the piece loses a dominant mood.
Do not let every line become a joke.
Do not bury the readable conversation under noise.
guardrails: Treat sampled ingredients as prompts, not obligations.
Let one seed dominate and let the others act as interruptions.
Let one prompt exchange play cleanly.
Keep the final button short and legible.
Every data-like scene should still deliver a clear emotional beat.
selected_style.analogy: a model interface panicking about what it can no longer hold
selected_style.seed_note: Lead seed: Context Window Panic. Palette from Context Window Panic, typography from Terminal Nocturne, motion from Context Window Panic, transitions from Broadcast Fever Dream, audio from Context Window Panic.

thesis_starter: This video makes the code and its logic feel like a model interface panicking about what it can no longer hold.

scene_swarm.description: A browser frame already parked on the funniest or most loaded region of a page.
A metrics or dashboard scene where numbers replace feelings.
A still or almost-still frame with a blinking cursor or pointer.
An exchange between prompt, system, and response that escalates into contradiction.
A freeze-frame punch-in that isolates a detail for absurd emphasis.
A terminal scene where logs or prompts read like a confession.
A very short distortion burst that resets momentum.
A field of parameters, weights, or tiny values treated like a landscape.
Stacking warnings or errors that become emotional rather than technical.
A clean held browser or document frame that lets the audience actually look.
Words or tokens appear one at a time with confidence, probability, or status overlays.
A final metrics scene spikes or overloads as if the system can no longer stay composed.
All complexity disappears until only a cursor, prompt, or tiny status remains.

beat_sheet.label: hook
beat_sheet.direction: Open with the strongest absurd promise in under 1.5 seconds.
beat_sheet.material_note: Lead with the most readable funny token, log line, or error fragment.
beat_sheet.style_note: State Seed Remix Default immediately through palette, typography, and the first motion gesture.

beat_sheet.label: premise
beat_sheet.direction: Establish the source material or world clearly.
beat_sheet.material_note: Show enough code context that the later edits remain legible.
beat_sheet.style_note: Preserve the Seed Remix Default mood while still allowing source-specific invention.

beat_sheet.label: escalation-a
beat_sheet.material_note: Use code crops, logs, terminals, and cursor behavior as primary visual grammar.
beat_sheet.style_note: Preserve the Seed Remix Default mood while still allowing source-specific invention.

beat_sheet.label: escalation-b
beat_sheet.direction: Mutate the motif through a different visual system.
beat_sheet.material_note: Use code crops, logs, terminals, and cursor behavior as primary visual grammar.
beat_sheet.style_note: Preserve the Seed Remix Default mood while still allowing source-specific invention.

beat_sheet.label: break
beat_sheet.direction: Insert a calm or deadpan reset.
beat_sheet.material_note: Use a blank terminal, idle cursor, or one-line log as the reset.
beat_sheet.style_note: Keep the reset beat clean enough that the Seed Remix Default identity stays visible.

beat_sheet.label: escalation-c
beat_sheet.direction: Bring in a second-wave interruption or invented support scene.
beat_sheet.material_note: Use code crops, logs, terminals, and cursor behavior as primary visual grammar.
beat_sheet.style_note: Preserve the Seed Remix Default mood while still allowing source-specific invention.

beat_sheet.label: collapse
beat_sheet.direction: Peak with the densest intentional composition.
beat_sheet.material_note: Use code crops, logs, terminals, and cursor behavior as primary visual grammar.
beat_sheet.style_note: Peak with signal loss cards and a stronger pass of server hum without losing readability.

beat_sheet.label: button
beat_sheet.material_note: Finish on an error, prompt, or absurd callback variable.
beat_sheet.style_note: Let the ending feel like a distilled final gesture of Seed Remix Default.

audio_plan.voice_mode: one main voice plus optional second system whisper
processing_defaults: subtle pitch drop or stutter on selected words
dropout or mute after one key line
mix_guardrails: Do not turn the whole piece into harsh distortion.

personal_spin_prompts: What is the video actually claiming about the material?
Which repeated line or symbol can carry that claim across multiple scenes?
What final button would feel authored rather than generic?
Which log line, comment, or token feels most emotionally reusable?
editing_rules: Front-load the strongest absurd promise in the first 1.5 seconds.
Bias toward more scenes than a straightforward edit would have.
Use bridge scenes to increase richness when source material is thin.
Keep most anchor scenes readable for at least 1.2 seconds.
Include one break scene with relative stillness.
Let one recurring motif mutate across the runtime.
Use no more than two font families in the final render.
Normalize the final mix and avoid clipped peaks.
Prefer restrained palettes, editorial contrast, and crisp typography.
Avoid bubble fonts, candy palettes, sticker-board clutter, and constant rainbow glitch.
Use full-frame noise, heavy blur, or shake only as accents.
Let at least one scene be almost pristine so later damage feels intentional.
Treat sampled ingredients as prompts, not obligations.
Let one seed dominate and let the others act as interruptions.
Let one prompt exchange play cleanly.
Keep the final button short and legible.
Every data-like scene should still deliver a clear emotional beat.
Do not let the seed remix become a checklist of borrowed tricks.
Do not sample so many ingredients that the piece loses a dominant mood.
Do not let every line become a joke.
Do not bury the readable conversation under noise.
ffmpeg_hints: Pre-render brittle scenes as short clips or frame sequences, then assemble with ffmpeg.
Prefer concat, overlay, and short crossfades over one giant brittle filtergraph.
Apply loudness normalization in the final pass: loudnorm=I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11.
If generating frames in Python, encode the final video with ffmpeg rather than writing video directly from Python.
quality_checks: The opening frame is immediately legible and compelling.
The video communicates one clear thesis or emotional claim.
The selected blueprint is visible in color, type, motion, and sound.
Scene count is high enough that the piece feels rich instead of flat.
At least one scene provides stillness or deadpan contrast.
Captions remain readable on a laptop screen at 100% size.
The palette feels restrained rather than randomly saturated.
Audio peaks are controlled and speech is understandable.
The final 2 seconds land a decisive button.
